explanation_md: The bare `throw;` re-propagates the original `D` object intact — no slicing occurs. The outer catch for `const D&` matches and prints `"D"`. If `throw e;` had been used instead, it would create a new `std::runtime_error` and slice away the `D` part.

`throw e;` creates a new exception of type `std::runtime_error` (the static type of `e`), slicing off the `D` part. The outer `catch (const D&)` does not match, so the `catch (const std::runtime_error&)` fires and prints `"runtime"`.

prompt_md: When is it valid to use a bare `throw;` statement?

options.a: Any time, as long as the program has previously thrown at least one exception
options.b: Only inside a `catch` block, where it re-throws the currently active exception
options.c: Only at the top level of `main`
options.d: Whenever a `try` block is in scope, even outside a `catch`

explanation_md: A bare `throw;` re-propagates the currently active (caught) exception. It is only valid inside a `catch` block or in a function called from a `catch` block while that exception is still active. Using `throw;` outside an active catch context is undefined behavior.

prompt_md: What is the difference between `throw;` and `throw e;` inside a catch clause?

options.a: They are identical — both re-throw the original exception object
options.b: `throw;` re-propagates the original exception preserving its dynamic type; `throw e;` creates a new exception of `e`'s static type, potentially slicing it
options.c: `throw e;` preserves the type; `throw;` always rethrows as `std::exception`
options.d: `throw;` terminates the program; `throw e;` propagates normally

explanation_md: `throw;` re-propagates the original exception object without copying or slicing — the dynamic type is preserved. `throw e;` makes a copy of `e` using `e`'s static (declared) type, which slices if `e` is a base-class reference to a derived object.
